It is based on the fact that the targums often translate Hebrew לקח with this verb when the object is a person. The targum's intent is to specify that לקח in such situation means "to lead away an animate being." Translating "to take" in such situations is quite wrong.

entries.end_notes: In JLA this word may occur under the vocalization as a G participle, q.v.
Originally probably a phonetic variant of tḥl "cress", then a common NENA word.

JLAtg usually uses this form for "fear" or "faith" but dḥl for a worshipped object; note, though, that the absolute form of this word is identical to the determined form of the latter. Thus confusion in the texts is rather rampant, especially in LJLA rendering the Hebrew form אלהים (divinity of the Northern Kingdom) where the text attempts to reflect the apparent plural ending in what is a pl. absolute of this word rather than some form of dḥl n.m.

entries.form_section: OA, OfA zy; MA dy, d; later d but also dy in various positions; Sam. often )d ; in Mandaic written with a unique ligature developed from "d" + "y", in our code transcribed as "á¸" (in Roman as "D"), and considered to be the last letter of the alphabet.
entries.end_notes:
Note that in PTA the full form די is almost exclusively reserved for the conjunction (with only 17 examples as a preposition) whereas the proclitic form may be either prepositional or conjunctional.
As a replacement for the construct genitive, the usage is extremely rare and clearly context driven in the older dialects.

entries.form_section: OA, OfA zy; MA dy, d; later d but also dy in various positions; Sam. often )d ; in Mand. written with a unique ligature developed from "d" + "y" and in our code transcribed as "d" with underscore. In some JBAmb also spelled as presumably pronounced: t .
entries.end_notes:
For a complete list of the exxs. introducing direct speech in the BT see J. N. Epstein, "Notes on Post-Talmudic Aramaic Lexicography", JQR 12(1922): 305.
All meanings of this particle, including the prepositional (see dy prep.) derive from meaning 2, fundamental to Semitic.
For the general tendency within Aramaic to expand the use ofdy/d--clauses for adverbial subordination see most recently N. Pat-El, Studies in the Historical Syntax of Aramaic (Gorgias, 2012), ch. 2.
The otherwise common usage introducing object clauses is rare in targumic texts, where BH כִּי is regularly rendered by ארי or ארום instead.

entries.end_notes: Latin delator "informer", or the like; for the argument for distinguishing this from dlṭwr see discussion Levy s.v.; but we are obviously not absolutely convinced of the distinction. The word is normally used to render Biblical חנף even though there is a perfectly good Aramaic cognate available. The reference would seem to be not just to pagans but to those aggressively so. See TargJob 15:35, which describes the band of such people as speakers of falsehood and deceit. As pointed out by R. Weiss (The Aramaic Targum of Job [1979]) p. 80, the basis of the usage would appear to be the Rabbinic Hebrew text of Midrash Breshit Rabba: התחיל אומר דילטוריה לבוראו.

entries.form_section: There are some possible examples in CPA confused with dyn pron. instead of normative dy conj./adv.
entries.end_notes:
See also s.vv. dy adv., dyn pron.
, and ˀykdyn adv.
From an etymological perspective, this ubiquitous Syriac enclitic is almost certainly the heir to earlier ˀdyn (rather than a similar but limited usage of the pronoun dyn in non-Syriac literary texts, which itself is rather a reflection of Biblical Hebrew usage), but in Syriac usage it is almost a total calque of Greek δέ. Also see s.v. hydyn. The whole issue is complex. See Butts, A.M., "Between Aramaic *ˀiδayn and Greek δέ: The Linguistic History of Syriac den," in Teule, H.//et. al, Syriac in Its Multi-Cultural Context (Louvain: Peeters, 2017) Pp. 13-30.
Note that there are LJLA contexts where the Aramaic lmh dyN is equivalent to BH לָמָּה alone rather than לָמָּה זֶּה.
Omitted from DCPA as the Syriac form of native

entries.end_notes: In Syriac the adverbial nature of this form is still retained inasmuch as it is possible for it to be followed by an independent personal pronoun, thus, e.g. ܕܠܐ ܐܢܬܝ, "without you", as well as with other adverbial expressions such as ܒܡܠܟܐ, "taking counsel". The straightforward prepositions are bl(d and (with somewhat of a causal connotation, i.e. "due to being without") mn bly. .

The frequency of the use of this form in LJLA instead of the expected dḥwl, demonstrates its authenticity, although whether it should be taken to be a derivative of the common verb dlḥ "to fear" rather then a metathesized from from dḥl "to disturb" remains to be seen. We have cited both roots as potential base forms.
There is also a great deal of confusion in the late mss. among the potentially homographic forms of n.m., nom.ag., and dlwxy, dlwxyˀ n.m.

entries.end_notes:
In JLAtg for biblical זֶה when it means "now".
The Geonic/LJLA usage surely is a hypercorrection by misunderstanding of the literary form as being equivalent to דנא. Only in the expression יומא דנן, i.e. as an expression of time, might the form be correct in the Babylonian literary dialect, otherwise it should be viewed as archaic and highly formulaic, possibly reflecting the relatively common usage with personal names in the magic bowl tradition.

entries.end_notes: The usage in the sense of "to be exacting" in Jewish texts is Mishnaic. DJBA may be correct in connecting it directly with dwq "to observe (carefully?)" rather than dqq "to crush" from the perspective of JBA, but given the antiquity of the related form dqdq "small" (an elative of dq "thin"), we suspect that the semantic relationship proposed by DJBA actually goes the other way, i.e., that both verbs dwq and dqdq are ultimately and separately derived from dq, whose root, of course, is dqq.

There is a great deal of confusion with the roots dšš and dwš in the existing lexica. Jastrow combines them. DJPA incorrectly includes 'to crush,pound' here. DJBA and LS2 incorrectly separate the G and C usages of this verb into two lemmas. PS and Audo (following BarBahlul) treat as pael of ˀdš.
